Siblings: Four brothers and one sister

What it means to represent the United States: It’s an honor to play the sport I love, alongside very talented teammates from all over the country, all while wearing USA across our chest!

Her role models in the game:Michele Smith – we are both lefty pitchers and number 32. I didn't realize she was #32 until after the fact I chose the number

Quick Facts
  • Son of Julie Wilhelmi and David Nichols
  • The youngest player on the Paralympic Winter Games Beijing 2022 sled hockey roster at 17 years old
Biographical Information

Evan Nichols was born with Arthrogryposis, a condition that affected his joints in his knees and ankles. At age five, he was diagnosed with Bilateral Perthes, which affected both of his hips, and he had to learn how to walk seven times as a child due to multiple surgeries to his legs. He loved sports, trying soccer, swimming and archery, before a childhood friend, Alex Green, suggested he join his sled hockey team based in Washington, D.C.

With his rink closed down during the COVID-19 pandemic, his parents would drive Evan four hours to play with the Pittsburgh Mighty Penguins out of Cranberry Township, Pennsylvania. That experience help mature his game, and Evan would make his national team debut in 2022 at age 17, becoming the youngest player on the team on the gold medal-winning U.S. sled hockey team at the Paralympic Winter Games Beijing 2022. 

Nichols is considered a “rat player” by his teammates, a player who can score a lot of goals in the creases and causes opponents to lose the puck because of his quick sticks.

bio.education: Farmington High School ‘01 University of Arizona '06, Special Education Rehab and School Psychology; University of Alabama '08, Kinesiology (Master’s)
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Alana Nichols is a three-sport phenomenon in wheelchair basketball, alpine skiing and paracanoe
  • She grew up in Farmington, N.M., and was an avid snowboarder throughout her youth. In 2002, however, Nichols landed back-first on a rock while attempting a backflip on her snowboard. The injury caused her to be paralyzed from the waist down
  • Nichols didn’t let her athletic career stop there, though, as she began playing wheelchair basketball just two years later and helping Team USA to win gold at the Paralympic Games Beijing 2008.
  • She is the first U.S. female Paralympian to win gold medals at a Summer and Winter Paralympic Games.
Paralympic Experience
  • 5-time Paralympian; 6-time Paralympic medalist (3 gold, 2 silver, 1 bronze)
    • Paralympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, 7th (Kayak single KL2 200m - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Sochi 2014, (Super-G - Sitting - Women), silver (Downhill - Sitting - Women), 4th (Giant Slalom - Sitting - Women)
    • Paralympic Games London 2012, 4th (Team - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Vancouver 2010, gold (Downhill - Sitting - Women, Giant Slalom - Sitting - Women), silver (Super-G - Sitting - Women), bronze (Super Combined - Sitting - Women), 8th (Slalom - Sitting - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Beijing 2008, gold (Team - Women)
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2016 – 8th (Kayak single KL2 200m - Women)
  • Years of participation: Kayak single KL2 200m - Women 2015, 2016; Downhill - Sitting - Women 2011, 2013; Giant Slalom - Sitting - Women 2011, 2013; Slalom - Sitting - Women 2011, 2013; Super Combined - Sitting - Women 2011, 2013; Super-G - Sitting - Women 2011, 2013; Team - Women 2006
  • Medals: 5 (2 gold, 1 silver, 2 bronze)
    • Gold – 2011 (Downhill - Sitting - Women, Super-G - Sitting - Women)
    • Silver – 2006 (Team - Women)
    • Bronze – 2011 (Giant Slalom - Sitting - Women, Slalom - Sitting - Women)

GET TO KNOW BUBBA NICKLES

Personal Motto: Never stress, just do your best, and trust God with the rest

Three words her friends would use to describe her: dorky, excited and passionate

If she wasn't playing softball she would be: I would probably be hosting my own television show about interior decorating because I love everything about interior design on HGTV

What it means to play for USA Softball: "It means that I get to play for the best country in the entire world and live out my childhood dream of playing for the same three letters that so many of the greatest softball players in history played for as well."

  • Position: Push Athlete
  • Son of Dan and Janet Niederhofer
  • Played wide receiver at Texas A&M University
  • His father, Dan, played football for Abilene Christian University and in the USFL for the Birmingham Stallions
  • Graduated with a degree in petroleum engineering
Biographical Information

Boone Niederhofer grew up in San Antonio, playing baseball and football through high school. He was the team captain and MVP of his high school football team and walked on to Texas A&M as a wide receiver, where, as a sophomore, he was catching passes from future NFL quarterback Kyle Allen.

After Niederhofer's football career ended, he worked in the oil and gas industry. He attended a bobsled combine on Texas A&M’s campus in the summer of 2019 and made his IBSF World Cup debut two years later with the U.S. bobsled team.

Niederhofer finished fourth in four-man bobsled at the 2025 IBSF Bobsled World Championships and was named to his first Olympic team for the Olympic Winter Games Milano Cortina 2026.

Eli grew up in the remote community of Stehekin Washington, in the North Cascade mountains at the head of Lake Chelan. Son of Robert and Tammy Nielsen with two older brothers, Will and Dylan.  Eli  attended a one-room school house through grade 7, then moved two valleys to the northwest to attend Liberty Bell High School and  join the Methow Valley nordic and biathlon teams in 2014.

Quick Facts
  • She most admires her mother, Vilma, because she is strong and determined

  • Was born without a left hand and has never found out exactly why, but doctors said the umbilical cord might have wrapped around her hand

  • Learned to play the piano at age 6

  • Her family moved from Queens to Kissimmee, Florida when she was 10 years old

  • Began playing sports in the 6th grade, including volleyball, track and field, and cheerleading

  • She earned Conference Player of the Year honors her senior year of high school

  • Coaches volleyball and is the director and founder of Limitless People Inc. Nonprofit.

  • During the summer, she works at a camp called Nuability Athletics, teaching sports to children ages 5 to 18 with physical disabilities
